content:
• The Double Standard Nobody Talks About: Why Women’s Reading Habits Are Judged So Harshly
A few months ago, I came across a short video.
A girl was talking about a romance novel she loved. Nothing unusual. Just another reader sharing her favorite story online.
Then I checked the comments.
One line stood out:
“Women are basically reading porn.”
It wasn’t just a random opinion. It had hundreds of likes.
And honestly, that one sentence explains a much bigger issue.
Not about books.
Not even about romance.
But about how differently we judge men and women for enjoying the exact same things.
Let me explain.
I have friends in different parts of the world — some in the US, some in the UK, and some here in Pakistan. And I’ve noticed something interesting.
When a guy talks about watching a show like Game of Thrones, nobody questions him.
They don’t say, “Oh, you just watch it for the sex scenes.”
They talk about the story. The politics. The characters. The strategy.
But if a girl talks about reading a romance or fantasy book with emotional and physical intimacy, suddenly everything changes.
Now it’s “weird.”
Now it’s “too much.”
Now it’s reduced to one thing.
That difference isn’t small. It’s everywhere.
In countries like the US or UK, people openly consume all kinds of media — Netflix shows, movies, novels — many of which include romantic or explicit scenes. It’s normal.
In South Asian countries like Pakistan, things are a bit more complicated.
People still watch the same shows. They still consume similar content. But when it comes to women openly enjoying or talking about it, the reaction becomes judgmental.
Not always publicly.
But quietly.
Subtly.
And sometimes, very loudly — like that comment.
Here’s the reality most people don’t talk about:
Romance books are not just “spicy scenes.”
Most of them are 300 to 500 pages long.
Out of that, maybe 10 or 20 pages include physical intimacy.
The rest?
It’s storytelling.
It’s character growth.
It’s emotional connection.
It’s conflict, trust, heartbreak, healing.
If someone reduces all of that to just “porn,” it says more about their perspective than the content itself.
And here’s where it gets interesting.
Men consume similar content all the time.
Movies with romantic scenes.
TV shows with explicit moments.
Video games with sexual undertones.
But nobody labels them.
Nobody says, “You’re basically consuming porn.”
Why?
Because it’s normalized when men do it.
But questioned when women do it.
I remember talking to a beginner content creator from Lahore.
She wanted to start a book review page on TikTok.
But she hesitated.
Not because she didn’t know what to post.
But because she was worried about what people would say.
“What if people judge me?”
“What if they think I’m reading something wrong?”
That fear is real.
And it doesn’t just exist in Pakistan.
Even in Western countries, women creators get similar comments — just packaged differently.
Sometimes it’s jokes.
Sometimes it’s sarcasm.
Sometimes it’s disguised as “advice.”
But the message is the same:
“You shouldn’t enjoy this openly.”
And that’s the real issue.
It’s not about books.
It’s about control.
When women enjoy stories that focus on their emotions, their desires, their perspective — it challenges a very old mindset.
A mindset that says:
Women should be passive.
Women shouldn’t express desire openly.
Women shouldn’t choose what they enjoy without approval.
But things are changing.
Slowly, but clearly.
More women are talking about what they read.
More creators are building platforms around books, storytelling, and reviews.
More audiences are listening.
And the more this happens, the weaker that judgment becomes.
Here’s something practical if you’re a beginner — whether you’re in Pakistan, India, the US, or anywhere else:
If you enjoy something, don’t let random comments define it.
Because most of the time, those comments come from people who don’t even understand what they’re criticizing.
They see one clip.
One line.
One scene.
And they assume the whole story.
But real readers know better.
Stories are not built on one moment.
They’re built on journeys.
And romance, like any other genre, is just another way of telling a human story.
With emotions.
With connection.
With meaning.
So no, women are not “basically reading porn.”
They’re reading stories.
Just like everyone else.
The only difference is — now they’re no longer quiet about it.
And maybe that’s what actually makes people uncomfortable.
